rationale: Option A is correct because the figure demonstrates that the method can produce high-fidelity geometry (faithfully capturing facial structure and deformation) and high-fidelity texture (sharp, consistent details) simultaneously. The caption explicitly states: 'The sharp, consistently unwrapped texture details demonstrate the effectiveness of our dual-branch design.' This implies the branches work together to achieve high quality in both aspects. Option B is plausible but not supported by the figure; the text mentions efficiency elsewhere, not here. Option C is incorrect as the figure shows one texture per geometry. Option D is contradicted by the caption and the visual evidence of high quality.
grounding: The figure shows high-quality geometry and texture for each subject. The caption states: 'The sharp, consistently unwrapped texture details demonstrate the effectiveness of our dual-branch design.' The text in the 'Visualization of Reconstructed Geometry and Texture Map' section also emphasizes the joint advantage of explicit mesh and texture representation.

question: Why does the proposed method achieve faster reconstruction speed compared to Gaussian-based methods like LAM?

options.A: Because it uses a higher-resolution texture map (1024×1024) which reduces processing time.
options.B: Because it operates on a compact representation with only 5K vertices and 4K tokens, reducing computational load.
options.C: Because it leverages a learnable token grid that eliminates the need for iterative refinement.
options.D: Because it uses a single-layer MLP for geometric deformation, which is inherently faster.

rationale: The correct answer (B) is supported by the 'Reconstruction Speed' section, which explicitly states the method's efficiency stems from its 'efficient mesh-based representation: our model operates on only 5K vertices and a compact texture token grid (4K tokens)'. Distractor A is a SCOPE ERROR — the 1024×1024 texture is mentioned in implementation details but is not cited as a reason for speed. Distractor C is a CONFLATION — the token grid is used for texture, not to eliminate refinement. Distractor D is a SUBSTITUTION — the paper mentions two-layer MLPs, not single-layer.
source_text: Our method demonstrates significant computational efficiency, reconstructing a complete 3D avatar in only 0.7 seconds from a single input image. This substantial speedup stems from our efficient mesh-based representation: our model operates on only 5K vertices and a compact texture token grid (4K tokens) for cross-attention, whereas Gaussian-based methods must process orders of magnitude more primitives (20K or 80K) to reconstruct details...

question: What trade-off does the proposed method's design choice of using a compact token grid and low-vertex mesh imply for reconstruction quality?

options.A: It sacrifices high-frequency texture details like texts and tattoos, which Gaussian-based methods handle better.
options.B: It reduces the ability to generalize to out-of-domain inputs, as shown in Fig. 8.
options.C: It limits the method to only reconstructing faces without accessories, as seen in Fig. 7.
options.D: It increases memory footprint due to the need for a large learnable token grid.

rationale: The correct answer (A) is supported by the 'Reconstruction Speed' section, which notes that while the method is fast, Gaussian-based methods 'still fail on high-frequency textures like texts and tattoos' — implying the proposed method may also struggle with these, despite its efficiency. The paper does not claim it handles them better. Distractor B is a SCOPE ERROR — generalization to OOD inputs is discussed separately and not linked to the token grid. Distractor C is a DIRECTION ERROR — Fig. 7 shows accessories are reconstructed. Distractor D is a CONFLATION — the method reduces memory footprint, not increases it.
source_text: This substantial speedup stems from our efficient mesh-based representation: our model operates on only 5K vertices and a compact texture token grid (4K tokens) for cross-attention, whereas Gaussian-based methods must process orders of magnitude more primitives (20K or 80K) to reconstruct details but still fail on high-frequency textures like texts and tattoos.

question: How does the dual-branch design contribute to the quality of the reconstructed texture map, as evidenced by the results?

options.A: By enabling the model to generate texture maps without any input image, using only the geometry branch.
options.B: By allowing the texture map to be decoded from a 64×64 token grid into a 1024×1024 map, preserving fine details.
options.C: By ensuring consistent, unwrapped texture details that transfer the input appearance to the UV atlas.
options.D: By using a two-layer MLP for geometric deformation, which improves texture alignment.

rationale: The correct answer (C) is supported by combining the 'Visualization of Reconstructed Geometry and Texture Map' section, which states the texture map 'effectively transferring the input appearance to the UV atlas', and the caption of Figure 7, which notes 'sharp, consistently unwrapped texture details demonstrate the effectiveness of our dual-branch design'. Distractor B is a SUBSTITUTION — while the token grid and decoding are mentioned, they are part of the implementation, not the reason for consistent unwrapping. Distractor A is a DIRECTION ERROR — the method requires an input image. Distractor D is a CONFLATION — the MLP is for geometry, not texture alignment.
source_text: The corresponding texture map displays sharp, high-resolution details with consistent color and lighting, effectively transferring the input appearance to the UV atlas. ... Figure 7. Reconstructed geometry and texture map visualization. ... The sharp, consistently unwrapped texture details demonstrate the effectiveness of our dual-branch design.
